sections.conclusion: A person becomes a candidate when the Registrar of Voters certifies the person as a candidate after having verified the signatures on the sponsors certificate. There is nothing in the Political Reform Act preventing a former candidate from working as a paid campaign worker for a former opponent, provided such payments of $50 or more are disclosed. Both the candidate and independent contractor have responsibility to ensure required expenditure information is disclosed; if the candidate does not report, the contractor must ensure proper reporting occurs.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks when a person becomes a candidate (upon filing or upon certification), whether former candidates can work as paid campaign workers for opponents, and what duties independent contractors have to report expenditures when candidates fail to do so.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that candidate status begins upon registrar certification, former candidates may work for opponents with proper disclosure, and both candidates and independent contractors share responsibility for reporting expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains multiple letters from January 7, 1979 and January 18, 1979, with FPPC response dated February 23, 1979. OCR quality is poor with numerous scanning artifacts. Questions 6-8 are mentioned but some are outside FPPC jurisdiction (bribery questions referred to district attorney). Response covers questions 1-5 and 8 substantively.

CONCLUSION: A person becomes a candidate when the Registrar of Voters certifies the person as a candidate after having verified the signatures on the sponsors certificate. There is nothing in the Political Reform Act preventing a former candidate from working as a paid campaign worker for a former opponent, provided such payments of $50 or more are disclosed. Both the candidate and independent contractor have responsibility to ensure required expenditure information is disclosed; if the candidate does not report, the contractor must ensure proper reporting occurs.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Nancy Bradshaw's questions about campaign disclosure requirements, specifically addressing when candidate status begins, eligibility of former candidates as campaign workers, and reporting duties of independent contractors under the Political Reform Act.
